This repository serves as:

docs.title: Question
docs.text: What is the difference between HashMap, ConcurrentHashMap, and Hashtable?

docs.title: Answer
docs.text:
HashMap is a non-thread-safe implementation of the Map interface that stores data as key-value pairs. Internally it uses hashing to distribute entries across buckets, which allows O(1) average lookup performance.

ConcurrentHashMap is a thread-safe implementation designed for highly concurrent environments. Unlike Hashtable, it does not lock the entire map for every operation. Modern implementations use a combination of CAS operations and fine-grained synchronization, allowing multiple threads to read and update the map concurrently with much better scalability.

Hashtable is a legacy thread-safe implementation. Most operations synchronize on the entire map, meaning only one thread can effectively modify or access protected operations at a time. Because of this, it typically performs worse than ConcurrentHashMap under contention.

In modern applications, ConcurrentHashMap is generally preferred over Hashtable when thread safety is required.

docs.title: HashMap
docs.text:
When inserting a value:

map.put("John", 100);

HashMap:

  1. Calculates the key's hashcode.
  2. Maps the hashcode to a bucket.
  3. Stores the entry in that bucket.

When retrieving:

map.get("John");

the same hash calculation is performed, allowing the map to jump directly to the correct bucket.

This is why average lookup complexity is O(1).

docs.title: Hash Collisions
docs.text:
Multiple keys may end up in the same bucket.

Before Java 8, collisions were stored as linked lists.

Since Java 8, large collision chains can be converted into balanced trees, improving worst-case lookup performance.

docs.title: ConcurrentHashMap
docs.text:
ConcurrentHashMap improves concurrency by:

  • Using CAS operations for many updates.
  • Applying synchronization only where necessary.
  • Allowing mostly lock-free reads.

This significantly reduces contention compared to Hashtable.

docs.title: Hashtable
docs.text:
Hashtable synchronizes most operations using a single monitor.

Effectively:

public synchronized V get(...)

public synchronized V put(...)

Every operation competes for the same lock.

docs.title: Is HashMap always O(1)?
docs.text:
No.

Average complexity is O(1), but excessive hash collisions can degrade performance.

Before Java 8, worst-case complexity could become O(n).

Since Java 8, treeified buckets improve worst-case complexity to O(log n).

docs.title: Why is ConcurrentHashMap faster than Hashtable?
docs.text:
Hashtable uses a single lock for most operations.

ConcurrentHashMap allows multiple threads to access different regions of the map concurrently, significantly reducing lock contention.

docs.title: Can HashMap be used safely by multiple threads?
docs.text:
Not without external synchronization.

Concurrent modifications can cause:

  • Lost updates
  • Inconsistent reads
  • Internal structure corruption

For concurrent access, ConcurrentHashMap should be used.

docs.title: Key Points to Remember
docs.text:
• HashMap → Fast, non-thread-safe.
  • ConcurrentHashMap → Thread-safe, scalable, modern choice.
  • Hashtable → Thread-safe, legacy implementation, generally avoided.
  • HashMap uses buckets and hashing.
  • ConcurrentHashMap uses CAS and fine-grained synchronization.
  • Hashtable synchronizes the entire map.

docs.title: 2. What happens when executing String s = "hello";?
docs.text:
When the JVM encounters the string literal "hello", it first checks the String Pool.

  • If the string already exists, the existing instance is reused.
  • Otherwise, a new String object is created in the String Pool.

The variable s stores a reference to that pooled String.

docs.title: Why Strings Are Immutable
docs.text:
String immutability provides:

  • Thread safety
  • String Pooling
  • Hashcode caching
  • Security

Example:

String a = "hello";
String b = "hello";

Both variables reference the same String object.

docs.title: Heap
docs.text:
The heap stores:

  • Objects
  • Arrays

Characteristics:

  • Shared among all threads
  • Managed by the garbage collector
  • Configurable using -Xms and -Xmx

docs.title: Stack
docs.text:
Each thread has its own stack.

The stack stores:

  • Method frames
  • Local variables
  • Object references

Characteristics:

  • One stack per thread
  • LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)

docs.title: Metaspace
docs.text:
Metaspace stores class metadata such as:

  • Class definitions
  • Methods
  • Fields
  • Annotations
  • Inheritance information

Characteristics:

  • Stored in native memory
  • Outside the heap
  • Replaced PermGen in Java 8

docs.title: 4. What is the difference between == and equals()?
==
docs.text:
Compares:

  • Primitive values
  • Object references

Example:

String a = new String("abc");
String b = new String("abc");

System.out.println(a == b);

Output:

false

docs.title: equals()
docs.text:
Compares logical equality.

Example:

System.out.println(a.equals(b));

Output:

true

docs.title: Important Rule
docs.text: Whenever equals() is overridden, hashCode() must also be overridden.

docs.title: 5. What is a memory leak in Java?
docs.text: A memory leak occurs when objects are no longer needed but remain reachable from GC roots.

docs.title: Common Causes
docs.text:
• Static collections
  • Caches without eviction
  • ThreadLocal misuse
  • Unremoved listeners
  • Long-lived references

docs.title: Key Point
docs.text: Garbage Collection cannot remove objects that are still reachable.

docs.title: volatile
docs.text:
Provides:

  • Visibility guarantees
  • Ordering guarantees

Does not provide:

  • Atomicity

Example:

volatile int counter;
counter++;

The increment operation is still not thread-safe.

docs.title: synchronized
docs.text:
Provides:

  • Mutual exclusion
  • Visibility guarantees
  • Atomic execution of critical sections

Example:

synchronized(lock) {
    counter++;
}

docs.title: AtomicInteger
docs.text:
Provides lock-free thread-safe operations using CAS (Compare-And-Set).

Example:

AtomicInteger counter = new AtomicInteger();

counter.incrementAndGet();

docs.title: What is CAS?
docs.text:
CAS stands for Compare-And-Set.

Conceptually:

IF currentValue == expectedValue
    update value
ELSE
    fail

CAS is implemented using CPU-level atomic instructions such as:

CMPXCHG

on x86 processors.

docs.title: 7. What happens during a Full GC?
docs.text:
A Full GC performs garbage collection across the entire heap.

Typically includes:

  • Young Generation
  • Old Generation

Characteristics:

  • Stop-The-World pause
  • Application threads are paused
  • Memory is reclaimed

Frequent Full GCs may indicate:

  • Memory leaks
  • Insufficient heap size
  • Poor JVM tuning

docs.title: 8. What is a ClassLoader?
docs.text: A ClassLoader loads classes into JVM memory at runtime.

docs.title: Bootstrap ClassLoader
docs.text:
Loads core Java classes:

java.lang.String
java.util.List

docs.title: Platform ClassLoader
docs.text: Loads JDK platform libraries.

docs.title: Application ClassLoader
docs.text: Loads application classes from the classpath.

docs.title: Parent Delegation Model
docs.text:
Class loading follows this order:

Application
    ↓
Platform
    ↓
Bootstrap

Benefits:

  • Security
  • Prevents duplicate class loading

docs.title: 9. What happens when multiple threads modify the same HashMap?
docs.text:
HashMap is not thread-safe.

Possible issues:

  • Lost updates
  • Inconsistent reads
  • Corrupted internal state
  • Resize-related problems

docs.title: Concurrent Resize Problem
docs.text:
When a HashMap grows, it resizes and rehashes entries.

Concurrent modifications during resize can corrupt internal structures.

For concurrent access, use:

ConcurrentHashMap

docs.title: Optimistic Locking
docs.text:
Assumes conflicts are rare.

Typically implemented using:

@Version
private Long version;

Update process:

  1. Read current version
  2. Attempt update
  3. Verify version before commit

If the version changed:

OptimisticLockException

is thrown.

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
• Better scalability
  • No database lock held

docs.title: Disadvantages
docs.text: • Updates may need retries

docs.title: Pessimistic Locking
docs.text:
Assumes conflicts are likely.

Database rows are locked immediately.

Example:

SELECT *
FROM account
FOR UPDATE;

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text: • Prevents concurrent updates

docs.title: Disadvantages
docs.text:
• Lower throughput
  • Potential deadlocks
  • Longer lock durations

docs.title: ExecutorService
docs.text:
Manages and executes tasks using a pool of reusable threads.

Example:

ExecutorService executor =
        Executors.newFixedThreadPool(10);

executor.submit(() -> processPayment());

Benefits:

  • Thread reuse
  • Resource management
  • Better scalability

docs.title: ForkJoinPool
docs.text:
Designed for recursive divide-and-conquer workloads.

Process:

  1. Split large task (Fork)
  2. Execute subtasks in parallel
  3. Combine results (Join)

Example:

parallelStream()

often uses ForkJoinPool internally.

docs.title: Work Stealing
docs.text:
Each worker thread has its own task queue.

If a worker becomes idle:

Worker-1 queue -> empty
Worker-2 queue -> many tasks

Worker-1 can steal tasks from Worker-2.

Benefits:

  • Better CPU utilization
  • Improved load balancing

docs.title: CompletableFuture
docs.text:
Represents an asynchronous computation.

Example:

CompletableFuture<User> future =
    CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(
        () -> userService.getUser()
    );

Supports chaining:

future.thenApply(...)
      .thenAccept(...)
      .exceptionally(...);

Allows asynchronous workflows without blocking.

docs.title: Relationship
docs.text:
• ExecutorService executes tasks.
  • ForkJoinPool is a specialized ExecutorService.
  • CompletableFuture represents asynchronous computations and typically uses ExecutorService or ForkJoinPool underneath.

docs.title: 12. What is a deadlock?
docs.text:
A deadlock occurs when two or more threads permanently wait for resources held by each other.

Example:

Thread A:

Holds Lock A
Waiting for Lock B

Thread B:

Holds Lock B
Waiting for Lock A

Neither thread can continue.

docs.title: Detection
docs.text:
Common tools:

jstack <pid>
  • VisualVM
  • Java Mission Control
  • Thread Dumps

docs.title: Prevention
docs.text:
• Consistent lock ordering
  • Reduced nested locking
  • tryLock()
  • Lock acquisition timeouts

docs.title: 13. What is the difference between synchronized and ReentrantLock?
synchronized
docs.text:
Example:

synchronized(lock) {
    // critical section
}

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
• Simple
  • Automatic unlock
  • Less error-prone

docs.title: Disadvantages
docs.text: • Less flexible

docs.title: ReentrantLock
docs.text:
Example:

lock.lock();

try {
    // critical section
}
finally {
    lock.unlock();
}

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
Timed lock acquisition:

lock.tryLock();

Interruptible locking:

lock.lockInterruptibly();

Fairness:

new ReentrantLock(true);

Multiple Conditions:

Condition notEmpty;
Condition notFull;

docs.title: Disadvantages
docs.text:
• Must manually unlock
  • Easier to introduce bugs

docs.title: Condition Objects
docs.text:
Condition objects create separate wait queues.

Example:

  • Producers wait on notFull
  • Consumers wait on notEmpty

This avoids waking unrelated threads and improves efficiency.

docs.title: 14. How would you implement a thread-safe counter?
Using synchronized
docs.text:
public class Counter {

    private int counter;

    public synchronized int increment() {
        return ++counter;
    }
}

docs.title: Using AtomicInteger
docs.text:
public class Counter {

    private final AtomicInteger counter =
            new AtomicInteger();

    public int increment() {
        return counter.incrementAndGet();
    }
}

docs.title: High Throughput Systems
docs.text:
Preferred:

AtomicInteger

because it avoids lock contention.

For extremely high contention:

LongAdder

often provides better performance than AtomicInteger.

docs.title: Common Follow-Up Questions
docs.text:
• What is CAS?
  • How does ConcurrentHashMap work internally?
  • What is fine-grained locking?

docs.title: Interview Answer
docs.text:
Heap stores objects and arrays and is shared among all threads.

Stack stores method frames, local variables, and references and is private to each thread.

Metaspace stores class metadata and resides in native memory outside the heap.

docs.title: Heap
docs.text:
Stores:

  • Objects
  • Arrays

Managed by the garbage collector.

Shared between all threads.

docs.title: Stack
docs.text:
Stores:

  • Method calls
  • Local variables
  • References

Each thread has its own stack.

Follows LIFO behavior.

docs.title: Why are local variables thread-safe?
docs.text:
Each thread owns its own stack.

Local variables are not shared between threads.

docs.title: Metaspace
docs.text:
Stores:

  • Class definitions
  • Methods
  • Fields
  • Annotations
  • Bytecode metadata

docs.title: Is Metaspace part of the heap?
docs.text:
No.

Metaspace uses native memory outside the Java heap.

docs.title: What existed before Metaspace?
docs.text:
PermGen.

Java 8 replaced PermGen with Metaspace.

docs.title: Interview Answer
docs.text:
The String Pool is a special memory area used to store String literals.

If a String already exists in the pool, the JVM reuses it instead of creating a new object.

docs.title: Where is String Pool stored?
docs.text: In modern Java versions, the String Pool resides inside the heap.

docs.title: Why is String immutable?
docs.text:
• Thread safety
  • String pooling
  • Hashcode caching
  • Security

docs.title: Interview Answer
docs.text:
Full GC performs garbage collection across the entire heap.

Application threads are paused during the operation.

Frequent Full GC events usually indicate memory pressure or memory leaks.

docs.title: What is Stop-The-World?
docs.text: Application threads are temporarily paused while garbage collection is performed.

docs.title: Interview Answer
docs.text:
ClassLoaders load classes into memory at runtime.

Main ClassLoaders:

  • Bootstrap ClassLoader
  • Platform ClassLoader
  • Application ClassLoader

docs.title: Parent Delegation Model
docs.text:
A child class loader asks its parent to load a class first.

This prevents duplicate loading and improves security.

docs.title: What loads java.lang.String?
docs.text: Bootstrap ClassLoader.
